session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Woodbury, Devon
Content:  It is a commuter village and is primarily residential, since the majority of the workforce commute to Exeter.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Woodbury, Devon
Content:  The parish, which has a population of 3,466 recounted for the village alone of 1,605 at the 2011 Census, lies on the east bank of the Exe Estuary, has borders – clockwise from the estuary – with the district of Exeter (near to Topsham) and then the parishes of Clyst St George, Clyst St Mary, Farringdon, Colaton Raleigh, Bicton and Lympstone.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Woodbury, Devon
Content:  "Woodbury" is part of the electoral ward of "Woodbury" and Lympstone whose population at the 2011 Census was 5,260.

Title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996 film)
Content: The Hunchback of Notre Dame is a 1996 American animated musical drama film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation for Walt Disney Pictures.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996 film)
Content:  The 34th Disney animated feature film, the film is based on Victor Hugo's novel of the same name.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996 film)
Content:  The plot centers on Quasimodo, the deformed bell-ringer of Notre Dame, and his struggle to gain acceptance into society.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Hunchback of Notre Dame (1996 film)
Content:  Directed by Kirk Wise and Gary Trousdale and produced by Don Hahn, the film's voice cast features Tom Hulce, Demi Moore, Tony Jay, Kevin Kline, Paul Kandel, Jason Alexander, Charles Kimbrough, David Ogden Stiers, and Mary Wickes in her final film role.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Kirk Wise
Content: Kirk Wise (born August 24, 1963) is an American film director, animator and screenwriter best known for his work at Walt Disney Animation Studios.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Kirk Wise
Content:  Wise has directed such Disney animated films as "Beauty and the Beast", "", and "The Hunchback of Notre Dame".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Kirk Wise
Content:  He also directed the English language translation of Hayao Miyazaki's "Spirited Away".

Title: Hercules (1997 film)
Content: Hercules is a 1997 American animated musical fantasy comedy film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation for Walt Disney Pictures.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Hercules (1997 film)
Content:  The 35th Disney animated feature film, the film was directed by Ron Clements and John Musker.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Hercules (1997 film)
Content:  The film is loosely based on the legendary hero Heracles (known in the film by his Roman name, Hercules), the son of Zeus, in Greek mythology.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Hercules (1997 film)
Content:  The film also featured the first positive portrayal of African American women in a Disney animated film.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belle's Magical World
Content: Belle's Magical World (also known as Beauty and the Beast: Belle's Magical World) is a 1998 direct-to-video animated musical film produced by Walt Disney Television Animation, and released by Walt Disney Home Video) on February 17, 1998.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belle's Magical World
Content:  The film is a followup to the 1991 Walt Disney Pictures animated feature film "Beauty and the Beast", features the voices of David Ogden Stiers as Cogsworth, Robby Benson as The Beast, Gregory Grudt, who replaced Bradley Michael Pearce as Chip Potts, Paige O'Hara as Belle, Anne Rogers, who replaced Angela Lansbury as Mrs. Potts, Jerry Orbach as Lumiere.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belle's Magical World
Content:  The film features two songs performed by Belle, "Listen With Our Hearts" and "A Little Thought."

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belle's Magical World
Content:  This storyline is set within the timeline of the original "Beauty and the Beast" (after Christmas but before the fight against Gaston).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)
Content: Beauty and the Beast: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack is the official soundtrack album to the 1991 Disney animated feature film, "Beauty and the Beast".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)
Content:  Originally released on October 29, 1991, by Walt Disney Records, the album's first half – tracks 2 to 9 – generally contains the film's musical numbers, all of which were written by composer Alan Menken and lyricist Howard Ashman, while its latter half – tracks 10 to 14 – features its musical score, composed solely by Menken.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)
Content:  While the majority of the album's content remains within the musical theatre genre, its songs have also been influenced by French, classical, pop and Broadway music.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)
Content:  Credited to Various Artists, "Beauty and the Beast: Original Motion Picture Soundtrack" features performances by the film's main cast – Paige O'Hara, Richard White, Jesse Corti, Jerry Orbach, Angela Lansbury and Robby Benson – in order of appearance.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)
Content:  Additionally, the album features recording artists Celine Dion and Peabo Bryson, who perform a pop rendition of the film's title and theme song, "Beauty and the Beast", which simultaneously serves as the soundtrack's only single.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)
Content: Beauty and the Beast is a 1991 American animated musical romantic fantasy film produced by Walt Disney Feature Animation and released by Walt Disney Pictures.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)
Content:  The 30th Disney animated feature film and the third released during the Disney Renaissance period, it is based on the French fairy tale of the same name by Jeanne-Marie Leprince de Beaumont (who was also credited in the English version as well as in the French version), and ideas from the 1946 French film of the same name directed by Jean Cocteau.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)
Content:  "Beauty and the Beast" focuses on the relationship between the Beast (voice of Robby Benson), a prince who is magically transformed into a monster and his servants into household objects as punishment for his arrogance, and Belle (voice of Paige O'Hara), a young woman whom he imprisons in his castle.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)
Content:  To become a prince again, Beast must learn to love Belle and earn her love in return to avoid remaining a monster forever.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Beauty and the Beast (1991 film)
Content:  The film also features the voices of Richard White, Jerry Orbach, David Ogden Stiers, and Angela Lansbury.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Human Again (song)
Content: "Human Again" is a song originally written for, deleted from, and later restored to the 1991 Disney animated musical "Beauty and the Beast".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Human Again (song)
Content:  With music by Alan Menken and lyrics by Howard Ashman, "Human Again" was replaced during production of the original 1991 version of the film by "Something There", but retained and revised by Menken and new lyricist Tim Rice for the 1994 stage musical adaptation of "Beauty and the Beast".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Human Again (song)
Content:  A newly produced sequence featuring "Human Again" was added to the "Beauty and the Beast" animated film for its 2002 IMAX Special Edition and subsequent DVD, VHS, and Blu-Ray home releases.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Sleeping Beauty (1959 film)
Content: Sleeping Beauty is a 1959 American animated musical fantasy film produced by Walt Disney based on "The Sleeping Beauty" by Charles Perrault.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Sleeping Beauty (1959 film)
Content:  The 16th Disney animated feature film, it was released to theaters on January 29, 1959, by Buena Vista Distribution.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Sleeping Beauty (1959 film)
Content:  This was the last Disney adaptation of a fairy tale for some years because of its initial mixed critical reception and underperformance at the box office; the studio did not return to the genre until 30 years later, after Walt Disney died in 1966, with the release of "The Little Mermaid" (1989).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Disney Renaissance
Content: In the history of The Walt Disney Company, the Disney Renaissance refers to the era from 1989 to 1999 during which Walt Disney Feature Animation (renamed Walt Disney Animation Studios in 2006) experienced a creative resurgence in producing successful animated films based on well-known stories, which restored public and critical interest in The Walt Disney Company as a whole.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Disney Renaissance
Content:  During this era, the studio produced and released ten animated films: "The Little Mermaid" (1989), "The Rescuers Down Under" (1990), "Beauty and the Beast" (1991), "Aladdin" (1992), "The Lion King" (1994), "Pocahontas" (1995), "The Hunchback of Notre Dame" (1996), "Hercules" (1997), "Mulan" (1998) and "Tarzan" (1999).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Sword in the Stone (film)
Content: The Sword in the Stone is a 1963 American animated musical fantasy comedy film produced by Walt Disney and released by Buena Vista Distribution.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Sword in the Stone (film)
Content:  The 18th Disney animated feature film, it was the final Disney animated film to be released before Walt Disney's death.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Sword in the Stone (film)
Content:  The songs in the film were written and composed by the Sherman Brothers, who later wrote music for other Disney films like "Mary Poppins" (1964), "The Jungle Book" (1967), "The Aristocats" (1970), and "Bedknobs and Broomsticks" (1971).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belmont, Lyme Regis
Content: Belmont is a Grade II* listed country house near Lyme Regis in West Dorset, South West England.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belmont, Lyme Regis
Content:  The house was occupied for many years by the English novelist John Fowles, and is now part of the Landmark Trust.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content: Crag Hall is a country house located to the east of the village of Wildboarclough, Cheshire, England.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  It was built in 1815 by George Palfreyman, the owner of a textile printing works nearby.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  It has since been extended by the addition of large curved bow windows at each end of the entrance front.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  The house is constructed in brick-sized blocks of brown sandstone, with ashlar quoins and dressings.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  It is roofed in slate.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  The house is in two storeys.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  The entrance front has five bays.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  In the centre is a raised portico with four Ionic columns.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  It is approached from each side by a flight of steps.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  Its base is rusticated and contains three arched recesses.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  Above the portico is a window with an entablature.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  About the house, Figueirdo and Treuherz comment that "it has an imposing air of millstone grit solidity".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  The house is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  Associated with the house are three structures listed at Grade II.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Crag Hall
Content:  These are the gateway with its wing walls, the retaining wall to the garden terrace, and a wall and summer house in the garden.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content: Ruloe House is a country house located 1.75 mi to the east of Norley, Cheshire, England.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content:  It was built in about 1873 for the Wilbraham estate, and designed by the Chester architect John Douglas.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content:  It is constructed in red brick and has red tiled roofs.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content:  The house is decorated with strip pilasters.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content:  It is in two storeys, with a four-bay south front.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content:  On its garden side is a circular turret with a conical roof.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ruloe House
Content:  The house is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II listed building.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Infield House
Content: Infield House (also known as 'Infield Park', or simply 'Infield') was a large late-19th century country house located to the north of Barrow-in-Furness, Cumbria, England.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Infield House
Content:  Infield House was built adjacent to Abbey Road as a residence for businessman Samuel John Claye, the owner of Claye's Wagon Works.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Infield House
Content:  After Claye's death in 1886, the house and Wagon Works were sold on and later became a convalescent home.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Infield House
Content:  The facilities closure lead to Infield House falling into a state of disrepair and it was eventually demolished in the 1970s and replaced by a housing estate named Infield Gardens.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Infield House
Content:  The only remaining feature of Infield House is the boundary wall and gate piers which mark the entrance to the modern housing estate.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: John Fowles
Content: John Robert Fowles ( ; 31 March 1926 – 5 November 2005) was an English novelist of international stature, critically positioned between modernism and postmodernism.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: John Fowles
Content:  His work reflects the influence of Jean-Paul Sartre and Albert Camus, among others.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content: Holker Hall (pronounced Hooker) is a privately-owned country house located about 2km to the southwest of the village of Cartmel, Cumbria, England, a location previously in the historic county of Lancashire.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  It is "the grandest [building] of its date in Lancashire ...by the best architects then living in the county."

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  The building dates from the 16th century, with alterations, additions, and rebuilding in the 18th and 19th centuries.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  The 19th century rebuilding was by George Webster in Jacobean Revival style and subsequent renovations were by E. G. Paley.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  Hubert Austin had a joint practice with Paley by the 1870s and they both rebuilt the west wing after it was destroyed by a major fire in 1871, only a decade after Paley's previous work on the structure.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  The fire also destroyed a number of notable artworks.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  Holker Hall is Paley and Austin's "most important country house commission."

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  The architectural historian Nikolaus Pevsner expressed the opinion that the west wing is the "outstanding domestic work" of Paley and Austin.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  In 1970 the hall itself, together with its terrace wall, were designated Grade II* Listed buildings.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  The house stands in an estate of about 80 hectares, and is surrounded by formal gardens, parkland and woodland.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Holker Hall
Content:  Within the grounds are six structures listed at Grade II.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Rumah Cililitan Besar
Content: Rumah Cililitan Besar ("Cililitan Besar House"), also known as simply Cililitan Besar or Lebak Sirih, is a former Dutch colonial country house located in Cililitan, Jakarta.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Rumah Cililitan Besar
Content:  It was known in Dutch as Landhuis Tjililitan Besar.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Rumah Cililitan Besar
Content:  It is located next to the complex of Soekanto Indonesian National Police Hospital.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Rumah Cililitan Besar
Content:  The architecture style of the building is a prototype for a late 19th century Dutch country house style known as the transitional Dutch Indies style.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Stowe House
Content: Stowe House is a Grade I listed country house located in Stowe, Buckinghamshire, England.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Stowe House
Content:  It is the home of Stowe School, an independent school and is owned by the Stowe House Preservation Trust who have to date (March 2013) spent more than £25m on the restoration of the house.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Stowe House
Content:  Stowe House is regularly open to the public and can be explored by guided tour all year round or during the school holidays you can explore at your own pace with a multimedia guide.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Stowe House
Content:  The gardens (known as Stowe Landscape Gardens), a significant example of the English garden style, along with part of the Park, passed into the ownership of The National Trust in 1989 and are open to the public.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Stowe House
Content:  The parkland surrounding the gardens is open 365 days a year.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Stowe House
Content:  National Trust members have free access to the gardens but there is a charge for all visitors to the house which goes towards the costs of restoring the building.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content: Dukenfield Hall is a country house located between Knutsford and Mobberley in Cheshire, England.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  Now a symmetrical brick building, it originated in the late 16th or early 17th century as a small cruck-framed house, entered at one end.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  During the 17th century it was faced with brick, cross wings were added and the roof was heightened.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  The house was originally called Podmore House.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  Further additions were made to the house in the 19th and 20th centuries.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  It is constructed in plum-coloured brick with stone dressings, and has a stone-slate roof.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  The house is in two storeys plus an attic.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  Its entrance front is E-shaped, and has three projecting wings with gables.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  The house is recorded in the National Heritage List for England as a designated Grade II* listed building.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  Associated with the house are two structures listed at Grade II.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dukenfield Hall
Content:  These are the gate piers to the forecourt, and a barn.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belsay Hall
Content: Belsay Hall is a notable Regency style 1807 country house located at Belsay, Northumberland.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belsay Hall
Content:  It is regarded as the first British country house to be built in entirely in new, Greek revival style.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belsay Hall
Content:  It is a Grade I listed building.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Belsay Hall
Content:  It was built to supersede Belsay Castle and its adjoining earlier hall just a few hundred yards away, and part of the same estate.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content: Donald Shebib (born 17 January 1938, Toronto), often called Don Shebib, is a Canadian film director, writer, producer and editor.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  A graduate of UCLA film school, Shebib gained prominence and critical acclaim in Canadian cinema for his seminal 1970 movie "Goin' Down the Road", which combined narrative storytelling with Canadian documentary tradition influenced by the British.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  The low-budget film crew travelled around Toronto in a station wagon, supported by funding from the newly-formed Canadian Film Development Corporation.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  The movie was screened in New York and hailed by Pauline Kael and Roger Ebert.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  Kael wrote that the movie showed up the ostensibly forced sincerity and perceived honesty of the films of John Cassavetes.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  Shebib is the father of Noah "40" Shebib.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  Goin' Down the Road was digitally remastered as one of the key films in the Canadian film canon and was honoured with a screening at the Art Gallery of Ontario.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Donald Shebib
Content:  A sequel was shot in 2011, called "Goin' Down the Road Again," featuring some of the original cast members as well as a new generation of characters.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Richard Münch (sociologist)
Content: Richard Münch (born 13 May 1945 in Niefern near Pforzheim, Germany) is a German sociologist and, as of 2013, professor emeritus at the University of Bamberg.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Richard Münch (sociologist)
Content:  His primary field is sociological theory, in particular the work of Talcott Parsons.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Richard Münch (sociologist)
Content:  In the 1980s, he was instrumental in popularizing Parsons in Germany and defended his functionalist "grand theory" of action against competing approaches, such as rational choice and Niklas Luhmann's systems theory, which had been gaining ground since the 1970s.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dorian Gray (1970 film)
Content: Dorian Gray (Italian: "Il dio chiamato Dorian") aka "The Sins of Dorian Gray" is a 1970 movie adaptation of Oscar Wilde's novel "The Picture of Dorian Gray" starring Helmut Berger.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dorian Gray (1970 film)
Content:  The Italian title translates as "A God Called Dorian".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Wilhelm Burgdorf
Content: Wilhelm Emanuel Burgdorf (15 February 1895 – 2 May 1945) was a German general in the Wehrmacht during World War II, who served as a commander and staff officer in the German Army (Wehrmacht) (army).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Wilhelm Burgdorf
Content:  In October 1944, Burgdorf assumed the role of the Chief of the Army Personnel Office ("Heerespersonalamt") and Chief Adjutant to Adolf Hitler.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Wilhelm Burgdorf
Content:  In this capacity, he played a role in the forced suicide of Field Marshal Erwin Rommel.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Wilhelm Burgdorf
Content:  Burgdorf committed suicide in the "Führerbunker" on 2 May 1945 at the conclusion of the Battle of Berlin.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Alfred Jodl
Content: Alfred Josef Ferdinand Jodl (   ; 10 May 1890 – 16 October 1946) was a German general and war criminal during World War II, who served as the Chief of the Operations Staff of the Armed Forces High Command (Oberkommando der Wehrmacht).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Richard Münch (actor)
Content: Richard Heinrich Ludwig Münch (10 January 1916 – 6 June 1987), better known as Richard Münch, was a German actor, best known for portraying Alfred Jodl in "Patton" (1970).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Richard Münch (actor)
Content:  He also portrayed General Erich Marcks in "The Longest Day" (1962).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Last Days of Patton
Content: The Last Days of Patton is a 1986 made-for-television film sequel to the 1970 film "Patton", which portrays the last few months of the general's life.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Last Days of Patton
Content:  George C. Scott reprises the role of General George S. Patton, and Eva Marie Saint portrays Beatrice Patton, the general's wife.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Last Days of Patton
Content:  It was directed by Delbert Mann.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Johannes Steinhoff
Content: Johannes "Macky" Steinhoff (15 September 1913 – 21 February 1994) was a German general, NATO official and Luftwaffe fighter ace during World War II.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Johannes Steinhoff
Content:  He joined the West German government's Rearmament Office as a consultant on military aviation in 1952 and became one of the principal officials tasked with building the German Air Force during the Cold War.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Johannes Steinhoff
Content:  He became the German Military Representative to the NATO Military Committee in 1960, served as Acting Commander Allied Air Forces Central Europe in NATO 1965–1966, as Inspector of the Air Force 1966–1970 and as Chairman of the NATO Military Committee 1971–1974.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Johannes Steinhoff
Content:  In retirement, Steinhoff became a widely read author of books on German military aviation during the Second World War and the experiences of the German people at that time.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ludwig Beck
Content: Ludwig August Theodor Beck (29 June 1880 – 21 July 1944) was a German general and Chief of the German General Staff during the early years of the Nazi regime in Germany before World War II.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ludwig Beck
Content:  Ludwig Beck was never a member of the Nazi Party, though in the early 1930s he supported Adolf Hitler's forceful denunciation of the Versailles Treaty and belief in the need for Germany to rearm.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Ludwig Beck
Content:  Beck had grave misgivings regarding the Nazi demand that all German officers swear an oath of fealty to the person of Hitler in 1934, though he believed that Germany needed strong government and that Hitler could successfully provide this so long as he was influenced by traditional elements within the military rather than the SA and SS.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: From Denver to L.A.
Content: "From Denver to L.A." is a song sung by Elton John, appearing on the soundtrack of the 1970 movie, "The Games".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: From Denver to L.A.
Content:  The song was released as a single in the U.S. in July 1970, miscredited on the record label to "Elton Johns".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: From Denver to L.A.
Content:  The single was issued just as John's career was starting to take off, but was quickly withdrawn because both John and his then-current record company objected to its release.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: From Denver to L.A.
Content:  It is now an extremely rare collectors' item.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: I Am Become Death
Content: "I Am Become Death" is the fourth episode of the third season of the NBC science fiction drama series "Heroes" and thirty-eighth episode overall.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: I Am Become Death
Content:  It was written by Aron Coleite and directed by David Von Ancken.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: I Am Become Death
Content:  The episode aired on October 6, 2008.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: I Am Become Death
Content:  The title is a reference to a phrase from the "Bhagavad Gita" and was famously quoted by Robert Oppenheimer; "Now I am become Death, the destroyer of worlds."

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill Neighborhood House
Content: Lenox Hill Neighborhood House (the “Neighborhood House”) is a multi-service community-based organization that serves people in need on the East Side of Manhattan and on Roosevelt Island.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill Neighborhood House
Content:  Founded in 1894 as a free kindergarten for the children of indigent immigrants and as one of the first settlement houses in the nation, Lenox Hill Neighborhood House is the oldest and largest provider of social, legal and educational services on Manhattan’s Upper East Side.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill Neighborhood House
Content:  Each year, they assist thousands of individuals and families who range in age from 3 to 103, represent dozens of races, ethnicities and countries of origin and "live, work, go to school or access services" on the East Side from 14th Street to 143rd Street and on Roosevelt Island.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill Neighborhood House
Content:  Their clients include indigent families and the working poor who live in the East Side's housing projects and tenements or who travel to the Upper East Side to work in low-wage jobs such as cashiers, housekeepers, nannies and laborers; 10,000 seniors; and hundreds of mentally ill homeless and formerly homeless adults.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill Neighborhood House
Content:  They have five locations between 54th and 102nd Streets, offer programs at dozens of East Side locations; their headquarters is located on East 70th Street.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content: The Waterloo Public Library (West Branch) is a historic building located in Waterloo, Iowa, United States.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  The public library was established there in 1896.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  It operated out of two rented rooms, one on the east side of the Cedar River and other on the west side.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  The Carnegie Foundation agreed to grant the community $21,000 to build this building and a similar amount for the east side branch on April 11, 1902.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  Waterloo architect J.G. Ralston designed both buildings in the Neoclassical style.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  They were both dedicated on February 23, 1906.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  The single-story brick structure has a projecting entrance pavilion capped with a triangular pediment that is supported by Ionic columns.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  Also noteworthy are the corner piers that feature bands of brick squares set into the stone.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  In 1977 voters in Waterloo approved a $3,650,000 bond referendum to renovate the city's 1938 post office and federal building to house the library.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  The post office vacated the building in 1979 when it relocated.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  The old library building was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1983.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library (West Branch)
Content:  It now houses law offices.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 23 Beekman Place
Content: 23 Beekman Place is the address of an apartment building located between East 50th and 51st Streets in the Turtle Bay neighborhood at the far east side of Midtown Manhattan, New York City.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 23 Beekman Place
Content:  The building was re-designed by Paul Rudolph, an American architect and one-time dean of Yale University.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 23 Beekman Place
Content:  It is one of the last of his projects still standing in the city.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Chrysler Building
Content: The Chrysler Building is an Art Deco-style skyscraper located on the East Side of Midtown Manhattan, at the intersection of 42nd Street and Lexington Avenue in the Turtle Bay neighborhood.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Chrysler Building
Content:  At 318.9 m , the structure was the world's tallest building for 11 months before it was surpassed by the Empire State Building in 1931.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Helmsley Building
Content: The Helmsley Building is a 35-story building located at 230 Park Avenue between East 45th and East 46th Streets in Midtown Manhattan, New York City, which was built in 1929 as the New York Central Building, and was designed by Warren & Wetmore, the architects of Grand Central Terminal, in the Beaux-Arts style.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Helmsley Building
Content:  Before the erection of the Pan Am Building – now the MetLife Building – this building stood out over the city's second most prestigious avenue as the tallest structure in the great "Terminal City" complex around Grand Central.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content: The Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch is a historic building located in Waterloo, Iowa, United States.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  The public library was established here in 1896.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  It operated out of two rented rooms, one on the east side of the Cedar River and other on the west side.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  The Carnegie Foundation offered a grant of $30,000 to build a new library, but disagreements erupted over whether to place the building on the east side or west side of the river.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  They then agreed to grant $40,000 for a mid-river building, or the same amount for two buildings.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  In the end they agreed to grant the community $24,000 to build this building and a similar amount for the west side branch.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  Waterloo architect John G. Ralston designed both buildings in the Neoclassical style.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  Both were dedicated on February 23, 1906.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  The single-story Bedford stone structure was built over a raised basement.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  It is one of the few stone buildings in Waterloo.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  The building has a central portico with paired Ionic columns.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Waterloo Public Library-East Side Branch
Content:  It is part of a larger central mass that is oriented from front to back and sits across the lower hipped roof.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill
Content: Lenox Hill is a neighborhood on Manhattan's Upper East Side.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill
Content:  It forms the lower section of the Upper East Side, closest to Midtown.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill
Content:  The neighborhood ranges from East 60th Street to East 77th Street south to north, by East River to the east, and by Park Avenue to the west.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill
Content:  A significant portion of the neighborhood lies within the Upper East Side Historic District designated by the New York Landmarks Preservation Commission in 2013 and expanded in 2010.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Lenox Hill
Content:  The neighborhood is part of Manhattan Community Board 8.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: HSBC Tower (Midtown Manhattan)
Content: The HSBC Tower is a 30-story office building located at 452 Fifth Avenue overlooking the New York Public Library and Bryant Park, on the southwest corner of 40th Street and Fifth Avenue in Midtown Manhattan.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: HSBC Tower (Midtown Manhattan)
Content:  The building, completed in 1984, is 400 ft tall and has 30 stories.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: HSBC Tower (Midtown Manhattan)
Content:  Since April 2010 it is owned by Property & Building Corporation (PBC).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: HSBC Tower (Midtown Manhattan)
Content:  The firm bought the property from Midtown Equities and Israeli holding company IDB Group for $330 million in a lease-back deal.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: HSBC Tower (Midtown Manhattan)
Content:  Asking rents at this Class A building are over $100 per square foot, according to the New York Observer, which make it amongst one of 80 buildings in New York City achieving triple digit rents.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Robert Von Ancken
Content: Robert Von Ancken is a prominent New York City real estate appraiser, whose accomplishments include testifying in front of the Supreme Court to deter the construction of a building over Grand Central Terminal and establishing the value of the World Trade Center prior to the terrorist attacks on behalf of the insurance companies.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Robert Von Ancken
Content:  Throughout his career he has appraised more than 8,000 properties in and around New York City, including the Empire State Building, the Chrysler Building, Rockefeller Center and Columbia University.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Robert Von Ancken
Content:  He has also been referred to as one of the "nation's busiest experts on air rights", and has spoken and been quoted extensively on the topic.

Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content: Dan Hunt is an American football coach and former player.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content:  He is the head football coach at Colgate University located in the Village of Hamilton in Madison County, New York, and has held that position since 2014.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content:  Hunt played for Springfield College from 1988 to 1991 and was a three-year starter at tight end.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content:  Hunt graduated from Springfield in 1992 with a degree in physical education, and earned his master's degree in athletic administration from Springfield in 1995.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content:  He began his coaching career as an assistant at Springfield College before coming to Colgate in 1995.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content:  He has coached tight ends, running backs, and quarterbacks during his tenure and he was named associate head coach in 2010.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Dan Hunt (American football)
Content:  Hunt was named as the 29th head coach at Colgate upon the retirement of Dick Biddle following the 2013 season.

Title: Ted Karras Jr.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  He was most recently head football coach at Walsh University, a position he held from December 2012 to November 2016.

session_0.speaker: System

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

session_0.speaker: System

Title: Miami metropolitan area
Content: The Miami metropolitan area, also known as the Greater Miami Area or South Florida, is the 67th largest metropolitan area in the world and the eighth-largest metropolitan area in the United States.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami metropolitan area
Content:  It is entirely located in the southern portion of the U.S. state of Florida.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami metropolitan area
Content:  With 6,066,387 inhabitants as of 2016, the Miami metropolitan area is the most populous in Florida and second largest in the Southeastern United States.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Sport in Miami
Content: The city of Miami and the Miami metropolitan area are home to four major league sports teams — the Miami Dolphins of the National Football League, the Miami Heat of the National Basketball Association, the Miami Marlins of Major League Baseball, and the Florida Panthers of the National Hockey League.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Title: Hollywood, Florida
Content:  The average temperature is between 68 and 83°F.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

session_0.speaker: System

Title: Hollywood, Florida

Content:  Its boundaries stretch from I-95 and NE 2nd Avenue on the east, to NW 47th and NW 57th Avenues on the west, and from the Broward County line on the north, to 151st Street on the south.

session_0.speaker: System

session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content: Tidal flooding, also known as sunny day flooding or nuisance flooding, is the temporary inundation of low-lying areas, especially streets, during exceptionally high tide events, such as at full and new moons.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  The highest tides of the year may be known as the king tide, with the month varying by location.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  In Florida, controversy was created when state-level government mandated that the term "nuisance flooding" and other terms be used in place of terms such as sea level rise, climate change and global warming, prompting allegations of climate change denial, specifically against Governor Rick Scott.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  This amid Florida, specifically South Florida and the Miami metropolitan area being one of the most at risk areas in the world for the potential effects of sea level rise, and where the frequency and severity of tidal flooding events increased in the 21st century.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  The issue is more bipartisan in South Florida, particularly in places like Miami Beach, where a several hundred million dollar project is underway to install more than 50 pumps and physically raise roads to combat the flooding, mainly along the west side of South Beach, formerly a mangrove wetland where the average elevation is less than one meter (3.3 feet).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  In the Miami area, where the vast majority of the land is below 10 ft , even a one-foot increase over the average high tide can cause widespread flooding.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  The 2015 and 2016 king tide event levels reached about 4 ft MLLW, 3 ft above mean sea level, or about 2 ft NAVD88, and nearly the same above MHHW.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  While the tide range is very small in Miami, averaging about 2 ft , with the greatest range being less than 2 m , the area is very acute to minute differences down to single inches due to the vast area at low elevation.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  NOAA tide gauge data for most stations shows current water level graphs relative to a fixed datum, as well as mean sea level trends for some stations.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Tidal flooding
Content:  During the king tides, the local Miami area tide gauge at Virginia Key shows levels running at times 1 ft or more over datum.

session_0.text:

Content:  Although West Palm Beach is located approximately 68 mi north of Downtown Miami, it is still considered a principal city within the Miami metropolitan area, due to the solid urbanization between both cities.

session_0.speaker: System

Content:  The estimated population of the Miami metropolitan area, which includes all of Palm Beach County, was 6,012,331 people at the 2015 census.

Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content: Giuseppe Tominz was born in Gorizia as the second of eleven children of Ivano Tominz, an Italian dealer in ironware of distant Slovene origin, and his wife Maria Anna Giacchini, a native Italian woman of Udine.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  He was educated in an Italian-speaking environment.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  He attended a primary school run by Piarists in Gorizia, where he began to learn to paint in the third year.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  He received his first training as a painter from the local artist Karel Keber and probably also from the painter Franz Caucig.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  In 1809, he left for Rome with the help of Austrian Archduchess Maria Anna, sister of Emperor Francis I of Austria, and count Francesco della Torre.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  In Rome, he studied painting with Domenico Conti Bazzani and in the Scuola del Nudo at the Accademia di San Luca.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  In 1814, he was awarded a silver medal for his drawing "Study of the Apostople".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  Two years later, he married Maria Ricci and in 1818, have two son Augusto and Alfredo will become the first director of the Revoltella Museum in Trieste.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  In the same year, Tominz returned with his family to Gorizia.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Giuseppe Tominz
Content:  Of his early artistic works, several anatomical studies, sketches and one of his landscapes "View of Vietri, Rieti and the Salerno Bay" have survived.

Title: FirstEnergy Stadium
Content: FirstEnergy Stadium, officially FirstEnergy Stadium, Home of the Cleveland Browns, is a multi-purpose stadium in Cleveland, Ohio, United States, primarily for American football.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: FirstEnergy Stadium
Content:  It is the home field of the Cleveland Browns of the National Football League (NFL), and serves as a venue for other events such as college and high school football, soccer, and concerts.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

session_0.speaker: System

Content:  Since 2017, capacity is listed at 67,895.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: FirstEnergy Stadium
Content:  The stadium sits on 31 acre of land between Lake Erie and the Cleveland Memorial Shoreway in the North Coast Harbor area of downtown Cleveland, adjacent to the Great Lakes Science Center and Rock and Roll Hall of Fame.

Content:  The site was previously the location of Cleveland Stadium from 1931 to 1996.

Title: Mike Holmgren
Content: Michael George Holmgren (born June 15, 1948) is a former American football coach and executive, most recently serving as president of the Cleveland Browns of the National Football League (NFL).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Mike Holmgren
Content:  Holmgren began his NFL career as a quarterbacks' coach and later as an offensive coordinator with the San Francisco 49ers, where they won Super Bowl XXIII and XXIV.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Mike Holmgren
Content:  He served as the head coach of the Green Bay Packers from 1992 to 1998, appearing in two Super Bowls, and of the Seattle Seahawks from 1999 to 2008.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Mike Holmgren
Content:  Prior to his career in the NFL, Holmgren coached football at the high school and collegiate levels.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 2010 Cleveland Browns season
Content: The 2010 Cleveland Browns season was the team's 62nd season as a professional sports franchise and its 58th season as a member of the National Football League (NFL).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 2010 Cleveland Browns season
Content:  The team failed to break the longest playoff appearance drought in franchise history, a current streak of eight seasons without reaching the playoffs.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 2010 Cleveland Browns season
Content:  The team finished 5–11, matching its win total from the 2009 season and placed third in the AFC North.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 2010 Cleveland Browns season
Content:  This season marked the first season under the leadership of team president Mike Holmgren and general manager Tom Heckert.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 2010 Cleveland Browns season
Content:  It also marked the second season under head coach Eric Mangini.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: 2010 Cleveland Browns season
Content:  The Browns played all of their home games at Cleveland Browns Stadium in Cleveland, Ohio.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cleveland Browns relocation controversy
Content: The Cleveland Browns relocation controversy, sometimes referred to by fans as "The Move", was the decision by then-Browns owner Art Modell to relocate the National Football League (NFL)'s Cleveland Browns from its long-time home of Cleveland to Baltimore during the 1995 NFL season.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cleveland Browns relocation controversy
Content:  Subsequent legal actions by the city of Cleveland and Browns season ticket holders led the NFL to broker a compromise that saw the Browns history, records, and intellectual property remain in Cleveland.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cleveland Browns relocation controversy
Content:  In return, Modell was permitted to move his football organization to Baltimore where he established the Baltimore Ravens.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cleveland Browns relocation controversy
Content:  The Ravens are officially regarded by the NFL as an expansion team that began play in .

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cleveland Browns relocation controversy
Content:  The city of Cleveland agreed to demolish Cleveland Stadium and build a new stadium on the same site, and the NFL agreed to reactivate the Browns by the 1999 season through either an expansion draft or a relocated franchise.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cleveland Browns relocation controversy
Content:  The Browns were officially reactivated in 1998 through the expansion process and resumed play in 1999.

Title: Diane Ladd
Content: Diane Ladd (born November 29, 1932) is an American actress, film director, producer and author.

session_0.speaker: System

Title: Diane Ladd
Content:  She has appeared in over 120 film and television roles.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  For the 1974 film "Alice Doesn't Live Here Anymore", she won the BAFTA Award for Best Actress in a Supporting Role and was nominated for the Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Diane Ladd
Content:  She went on to win the Golden Globe Award for Best Supporting Actress on Television for "Alice" (1980–81), and to receive Academy Award nominations for "Wild at Heart" (1990) and "Rambling Rose" (1991).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Diane Ladd
Content:  Her other film appearances include "Chinatown" (1974), "Ghosts of Mississippi" (1996), "Primary Colors" (1998), "28 Days" (2000), and "American Cowslip" (2008).

session_0.speaker: System

Title: Diane Ladd
Content:  Ladd is the mother of actress Laura Dern, with her ex-husband, actor Bruce Dern.

Title: Octavia Spencer
Content: Octavia Lenora Spencer (born May 25, 1972) is an American actress and author.

Title: Octavia Spencer
Content:  She made her film debut in the 1996 drama film "A Time to Kill".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  Her breakthrough came in 2011, when she starred as Minny Jackson in the period drama film "The Help", for which she won the Academy Award, Golden Globe, SAG, BAFTA, and Critics' Choice Movie Award for Best Supporting Actress.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  She had a critically acclaimed performance in Ryan Coogler's drama "Fruitvale Station" (2013), for which she received the National Board of Review Award for Best Supporting Actress.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Title: Mask (film)
Content: Mask is a 1985 American biographical drama film directed by Peter Bogdanovich, starring Cher, Sam Elliott, and Eric Stoltz with supporting roles played by Dennis Burkley, Laura Dern, Estelle Getty, and Richard Dysart.

session_0.speaker: System

session_0.speaker: System

Content:  It describes the life of Alfred Charles Kinsey (played by Liam Neeson), a pioneer in the area of sexology.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  The single topped the "Billboard" Hot 100 in December 1974.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  As Chapin's only No. 1 hit song, it became the best known of his work and a staple for folk rock music.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Cat's in the Cradle
Content:  Chapin's recording of the song was nominated for the 1975 Grammy Award for Best Male Pop Vocal Performance and was inducted into the Grammy Hall of Fame in 2011.

Content:  It first appeared as the fourth track on the band's 1970 debut album, "Black Sabbath".

Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content: The Best of Black Sabbath is a double CD compilation album by Black Sabbath released in 2000 on the Sanctuary Records label.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content:  Its 32 songs are presented chronologically from the band's first 11 albums, spanning the years 1970 to 1983.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content:  Black Sabbath's classic six-album run, from 1970s debut "Black Sabbath" through 1975's "Sabotage" is celebrated with three to six songs from each album.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content:  Original vocalist Ozzy Osbourne's subsequent final two albums with the band, 1976's "Technical Ecstasy" and 1978's "Never Say Die!

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content: ", are represented by one and two songs, respectively.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content:  Replacement Ronnie James Dio's early 80's stint fronting the band on two albums is acknowledged with the title track of 1980's "Heaven and Hell" and a track from 1981's "The Mob Rules".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: The Best of Black Sabbath
Content:  The compilation closes with a song from 1983's attempted rebirth, "Born Again", former Deep Purple vocalist Ian Gillan's sole album with the band.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:

Content:  "The Best of Black Sabbath" does not include any later material with vocalists Glenn Hughes (1986's "Seventh Star"), Tony Martin (1986–96) or the returning Dio (1992's "Dehumanizer").

Title: Godspeed (band)
Content: Godspeed is an American metal band formed in 1992.

Content:  Following the release of their debut album Ride on Atlantic Records, produced by Rachel Bolan of Skid Row, the band embarked on two high-profile tours as the opening act for Black Sabbath and Dio.
