session_0.text:
Title: Kham Magar
Content: Western magar are descriptive terms invented by academic linguists and anthropologists for a nationality in the Middle Hills of mid-western Nepal inhabiting highlands extending through eastern "Rukum" and northern "Salyan", "Rolpa" and "Pyuthan" Districts in "Rapti"Zone as well as "Dhaulagiri" and "Bheri" Zones.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Kham Magar
Content:  They speak a complex of Tibeto-Burman distinct language called magar Pang.But there is no any confusion that magar Pang,magar kaike and magar dhut are same languages they have derived from Pali language.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Riadh Sidaoui
Content: Riadh Sidaoui (رياض الصيداوي) (born in Bou Hajla, 14 May 1967) is a Tunisian writer and political scientist who has a Swiss nationality.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: József Károly Hell
Content: Jozef Karol Hell (Slovak: "Jozef Karol Hell", German: "Josef/ph Karl Hell", Hungarian: "Hell József Károly") (15 May 1713, Szélakna (Windschacht, Piarg, now Štiavnické Bane) - 11 March 1789, Selmecbánya (Schemnitz, now Banská Štiavnica)) was a Hungarian mining engineer and inventor, who invented the water-pillar (water pump machine) in 1749 (first use 1753).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: József Károly Hell
Content:  It is mainly used today for oil extraction.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: József Károly Hell
Content:  He also proposed construction of the tajchy reservoirs around Selmecbánya (Schemnitz, Banská Štiavnica).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: József Károly Hell
Content:  He was a student of Sámuel Mikoviny in 1737.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: József Károly Hell
Content:  Regardless of his nationality, he is a pride of both Hungarian and Slovak nations.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Polish notation
Content: Polish notation (PN), also known as normal Polish notation (NPN), Łukasiewicz notation, Warsaw notation, Polish prefix notation or simply prefix notation, is a mathematical notation in which operators precede their operands, in contrast to reverse Polish notation (RPN) in which operators follow their operands.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Polish notation
Content:  It does not need any parentheses as long as each operator has a fixed number of operands.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Polish notation
Content:  The description "Polish" refers to the nationality of logician Jan Łukasiewicz, who invented Polish notation in 1924.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Reverse Polish notation
Content: Reverse Polish notation (RPN), also known as Polish postfix notation or simply postfix notation, is a mathematical notation in which operators follow their operands, in contrast to Polish notation (PN), in which operators precede their operands.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Reverse Polish notation
Content:  It does not need any parentheses as long as each operator has a fixed number of operands.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Reverse Polish notation
Content:  The description "Polish" refers to the nationality of logician Jan Łukasiewicz, who invented Polish notation in 1924.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Colorado River Bridge at Bastrop
Content: The Colorado River Bridge at Bastrop is a 1285 ft -long bridge with three steel truss spans and concrete piers that crosses the Colorado River as part of Loop 150 through Bastrop, Texas.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Colorado River Bridge at Bastrop
Content:  The three bridge spans over the river consist of identical Parker through trusses, each 192 ft in length, supported on concrete piers.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Colorado River Bridge at Bastrop
Content:  The bridge is one of the earliest surviving uses of the Parker truss in Texas.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Nashua River
Content: The Nashua River, 37.5 mi long, is a tributary of the Merrimack River in Massachusetts and New Hampshire in the United States.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Nashua River
Content:  It is formed in eastern Worcester County, Massachusetts, by junction of its north and south branches near Lancaster, and flows generally north-northeast past Groton to join the Merrimack at Nashua, New Hampshire.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Nashua River
Content:  The Nashua River Watershed occupies a major portion of north-central Massachusetts and a much smaller portion of southern New Hampshire.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content: The Port Mann Bridge is a 10-lane cable-stayed bridge that opened to traffic in 2012.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  It is currently the second longest cable-stayed bridge in North America and was the widest bridge in the world until the opening of the new Bay Bridge in California.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  The new bridge replaced a steel arch bridge that spanned the Fraser River, connecting Coquitlam to Surrey in British Columbia near Vancouver.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  The old bridge consisted of three spans with an orthotropic deck carrying five lanes of Trans-Canada Highway traffic, with approach spans of three steel plate girders and concrete deck.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  The total length of the previous Port Mann was 2093 m , including approach spans.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  The main span was 366 m , plus the two 110 m spans on either side.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  Volume on the old bridge was 127,000 trips per day.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  Approximately 8 percent of the traffic on the Port Mann bridge was truck traffic.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Port Mann Bridge
Content:  The previous bridge was the longest arch bridge in Canada and third-longest in the world at the time of its inauguration.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content: The Columbia River Bridge, also known as the Bridgeport Bridge, at Bridgeport, Washington was built to span the Columbia River in 1950.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content:  Composed of three spans, the bridge is a steel continuous riveted deck truss carrying Washington State Route 17 on a 26 ft wide roadway and two 3 ft sidewalks.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content:  The center portion of the bridge spans 300 ft , flanked by 250 ft end spans.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content:  The 70 ft north approach span and the 100 ft south approach span are supported by steel plate girders.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content:  The bridge played a significant role in the construction of the Chief Joseph Dam just upstream, as no bridge crossing had previously existed in the area.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content:  Construction on the dam started in 1945 and was completed, apart from the powerplant, in 1955.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Columbia River Bridge (Bridgeport, Washington)
Content:  The bridge was designed and built by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers as part of the Chief Joseph dam project, and is significant for its association with the project and as a major crossing of the Columbia.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content: The Miami Bridge, also known as the McDaniel Memorial Bridge, is a new concrete girder bridge that was built to replace a cantilever through truss bridge over the Missouri River at Miami, Missouri between Saline County, Missouri and Carroll County, Missouri.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  The Miami Bridge carries Route 41.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  The Miami Bridge was built in 1939, and its deck was replaced in 1983 as part of a rehabilitation project.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  The old bridge's main cantilever span was 474.7 feet, while the two anchor spans were each 415 feet in length, resulting in a total cantilever truss length of 1304.7 feet.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  There were 11 approach spans, including four Warren deck truss spans, three on the northern approach and one on the southern approach.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  All remaining approach spans were steel stringer (multi-beam/girder) spans.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  Total bridge length including approach spans is 2,071.9 feet.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Miami Bridge
Content:  The bridge's deck width is 23.0 feet and it has vertical clearance of 16.5 feet.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content: Built in 1890, the 2376 ft Walnut Street Bridge was the first to connect Chattanooga, Tennessee's downtown with the North Shore.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  According to a plaque on the bridge, Edwin Thacher was the chief engineer for the bridge.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  The bridge's superstructure was assembled by the Smith Bridge Company of Toledo, Ohio, which was a prolific late 19th-century bridge builder.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  The bridge's substructure was constructed by Neeley, Smith, and Company of Chattanooga.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  Most of the parts for the bridge were manufactured by Manly Jail Works of Dalton, Georgia and then shipped to the site by rail.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  The bridge's main spans are pin-connected Pennsylvania through truss spans.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  The top chord of these truss spans are configured in five sections, making the spans similar to the Camelback truss design.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Walnut Street Bridge (Chattanooga)
Content:  The bridge is historically significant as an extremely long and old example of its type; according to the Historic American Engineering Record: "The bridge was apparently the first non-military highway bridge across the Tennessee River."

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content: The Monocacy river bridge and viaduct is a 326 ft open deck steel girder bridge with two main spans crossing the river and 2 viaduct sections crossing the floodplain, south of Walkersville, Maryland.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  Originally constructed by the Frederick and Pennsylvania Line Railroad Company (F&PL).

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  Construction began in late 1871, and continued until July 1872 when the railroad opened that year.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  It was rebuilt by the Pennsylvania railroad prior first in 1900-1905 as an open deck riveted iron plate under girder bridge.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  In 1915, the bridge was surveyed as part of the Interstate Commerce Commission's ("ICC") effort to establish freight rates for the Parent railroad.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  In 1927, the Pennsylvania railroad rebuilt the bridge again using deeper and thicker steel girders, but leaving the masonry piers intact.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  In 1972, the two 85 foot river spans were washed out by Hurricane Agnes.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  In 1982, the State of Maryland purchased the bridge as part of the railroad line.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  In 1995, the State rebuilt the river spans and Pier 3 which is located in the middle of the river crossing was completely reconstructed using concrete to replace the original masonry foundations.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct
Content:  In 2015, the State performed minor maintenance on the structure and painted some spans.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text: Title: Monocacy River bridge and viaduct Content:

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content: The Maiden Lane Bridge was a railroad bridge across the Hudson River between the city of Albany and Rensselaer County, New York.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  It was designed by Kellogg, Clark & Co., and was one of the largest bridges they designed.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  The bridge was owned and built by the Hudson River Bridge Company, which was owned jointly by the New York Central and Hudson River Railroad Company which owned 3/4, and the Boston and Albany Railroad Company which owned 1/4.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  The Maiden Lane Bridge was often referred to as the "South Bridge", while the Livingston Avenue Bridge was referred to as the "North Bridge".

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  The Livingston Ave. Bridge was used for freight (and through-traffic passenger trains) while passenger trains used the Maiden Lane Bridge for access to Union Station, which was completed less than 10 months later.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  The state of New York authorized construction on May 10, 1869, construction began in May 1870, and the first train crossed on December 28, 1871.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  The bridge consisted of four 185.5 ft long fixed spans, one 274 ft long draw span, seven 73 ft long spans over the Albany Basin, one 110 ft long span over Quay Street, and one 63 ft long span over Maiden Lane.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  All the spans except the one over Maiden Lane were double tracked, through, and pin connected; the span over Maiden Lane was also double tracked, but was a deck and plate girder span.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  A reconstruction of the bridge, except for the draw span, was done in 1899 by Pencoyd Bridge Company and finished by January 3, 1900.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Maiden Lane Bridge
Content:  The bridge lasted until the 1960s, when the Albany-Rensselaer Amtrak Station was built on the east side of the Hudson in the city of Rensselaer and Interstate 787 was built along the west side in Albany, thereby eliminating the need of the bridge.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Atherton Bridge
Content: The Atherton Bridge is a historic iron truss bridge in Lancaster, Massachusetts, spanning the South Branch of the Nashua River.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Atherton Bridge
Content:  It is a rare example of a hybrid pony truss that is similar to the 19th century truss design of Simeon S. Post.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Atherton Bridge
Content:  It was built by J.H. Cofrode & Co. of Philadelphia in 1870.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Atherton Bridge
Content:  It was the first iron bridge to be constructed in the town.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Atherton Bridge
Content: <ref name="memory.loc.gov/cgi-bin/query/D?hh:1:./temp/~ammem_Pr5I::">Historic American Engineering Record</ref> The bridge was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1979.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Pit River Bridge
Content: The Pit River Bridge (officially the Veterans of Foreign Wars Memorial Bridge) is a double deck, deck truss, road and rail bridge over Shasta Lake in Shasta County, California.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Pit River Bridge
Content:  The bridge, carrying Interstate 5 on its upper deck and Union Pacific Railroad on its lower deck, was built in 1942 as part of the construction of the Shasta Dam/Shasta Lake reservoir system.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Pit River Bridge
Content:  The Pit River Bridge was constructed to replace the Lower Pit River Bridge, as the rising waters of the Shasta Lake reservoir would have put the older bridge underwater.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Pit River Bridge
Content:  The entire bridge spans 3588 ft long on the upper deck and 2754 ft on the lower deck.

session_0.speaker: System
session_0.text:
Title: Pit River Bridge
Content:  With a height of 500 ft above the old Pit River bed, it is structurally the highest double decked bridge in the United States; however, today the bridge sits only about 40 ft above the water when Shasta Lake is full.
